text.content:
This is a fifteen-minute on-ramp: two combatants, one back alley, one goal — see the SRX combat loop resolve end to end so the rest of the system makes sense. You do not need to know the rules to run it. The system does the arithmetic; you make the calls.

What you need first

  • Foundry VTT v14 with the SRX (Unofficial) system installed and a world created on it. (See the companion SRX GM Setup Guide if you have not done this yet.)
  • One player character. The fastest path is the Create / Advance button on a fresh character sheet — the priority wizard hands you a legal runner in a couple of minutes. A hand-built sheet works just as well.
  • One opponent. Import a threat from the catalog, or make a second character and treat it as the bad guy. Anything with an attack and a health track will do.

No rules text is bundled with this system. Playing SRX requires the free SRX rulebook from the SRX team; this guide only teaches the Foundry tooling.

The scene in one line

Your runner rounds a corner into a dead-end alley and a waiting ganger opens up. Fight or talk — but this walkthrough assumes you fight.

1. Make a scene

Create any Scene (a blank battlemap is fine) and activate it. Grid units default to metres, which is what SRX ranges assume — you do not need to change anything.

2. Drop two tokens

Drag your runner and the ganger onto the canvas a short distance apart. Each token's bars show the two SRX condition monitors: Physical (primary) and Stun (secondary). Watching those bars fall is how you read the fight at a glance.

3. Add both to the Combat Tracker

Select both tokens and click the Toggle Combat State sword, or right-click a token and add it to combat. They now appear in the tracker on the right.

Tip: keep the character sheets open side by side. The sheet's Roll buttons — attributes, skills, and each equipped weapon — are your main controls once the shooting starts.

SRX uses multi-pass initiative: fast characters act more than once per Combat Turn. The system handles this for you.

Roll it

Click Roll All (or roll each combatant) in the Combat Tracker. SRX overrides Foundry's default formula, so each combatant gets an initiative score from their own attributes plus initiative dice — no house formula to type in.

Read the passes

  • Everyone acts in descending initiative order. That is Pass 1.
  • When the pass ends, the system re-evaluates: any combatant whose score is still above zero acts again in Pass 2, and so on. A wired-up samurai may take two or three actions before a plain ganger takes their second.
  • Advance with the tracker's Next Turn / Next Round controls. Scores re-roll at the start of each new Combat Turn automatically.

You do not track any of this by hand — just keep clicking Next and act on whoever is highlighted.

Here is one full exchange. The pattern — attack → defend → resist → apply — is the spine of every SRX fight.

1. The attacker shoots

On the runner's turn, click the equipped weapon's Roll button on the sheet. A pre-roll dialog opens: confirm range, called shot, recoil, and any situational modifiers, then roll. A chat card reports the hits.

2. The defender answers

The attack card offers the target a defence roll. The defender (or the GM on their behalf) rolls to dodge; the card compares hits and shows whether the shot lands and by how much.

3. The target resists damage

If the shot connects, the card produces a damage-resistance button authored for the defender. Rolling it soaks part of the incoming damage with Body and armour.

4. Apply the result

The final card carries an Apply button. Because damage crosses ownership boundaries, the system routes the change through a GM executor so it works even when a player targets the GM's ganger — the ganger's Physical (or Stun) monitor drops, and the token bar updates.

Automation knobs: under Game Settings → Configure Settings → SRX you can dial how much the system does for you — from full auto-apply to manual, roll-only prompts. Leave it on the defaults for this fight.

5. Repeat

The ganger returns fire on their turn using the same four steps. Run passes until one monitor fills up.

Spending Edge

Every roll dialog exposes the SRX Edge options — buy automatic hits, push extra dice, re-roll, and so on. When a shot absolutely has to land, spend Edge from the dialog before you roll; the chat card itemises what you spent.

Glitches

Watch the chat card's dice readout. A glitch (a swarm of ones) flags on the card even on a successful roll; a critical glitch means something goes wrong regardless of hits. Narrate the jam, the ricochet, the dropped clip — the card tells you when.

Downing a combatant

When a monitor fills, the target is out — unconscious on Stun, dying on Physical. Overflow, dying countdowns, and first-aid all have system support:

  • Open the downed sheet and use First Aid / Stabilise; like damage, healing routes through the GM executor so a medic can patch a teammate they do not own.
  • In-combat world time advances with the round flow, so bleed-out and similar timers tick on their own.

You did it

That is the whole loop. Everything else in SRX — magic, the Matrix, vehicles, character advancement — hangs off this same declare → roll → resist → apply rhythm. When you are ready for more:

  • Magic: cast from the sheet's Magic tab; drain, sustaining penalties, and spirits are automated.
  • Matrix: a persona/hacking loop with programs, marks, devices, and technomancy on the Matrix tab.
  • Vehicles & drones: a cockpit sheet plus a chase tracker that resolves range each turn.
  • Advancement: the Create / Advance button's Karma panel spends Karma on attributes, skills, and talents with a running ledger.

See the SRX GM Setup Guide for install, catalog import, and character creation details.
